questions.question: What is a 'Feature' in the context of this lesson?

options.text: The emotional transformation of the customer
A factual statement about what the product is or has
The amount of money a customer saves using the product
A marketing tagline used on the homepage

questions.explanation: A Feature is a technical reality or factual statement (e.g., '128-bit encryption'). It describes what the product is, but not yet why it matters.

questions.question: Which question helps you translate a Feature into a Benefit?

options.text: How much does this cost?
So what?
Now what?
Who is the hero?

questions.explanation: Asking 'So what?' forces you to explain the functional advantage of a feature. (e.g., Feature: 'Real-time sync' → So what? → Benefit: 'Your team always sees the latest updates.')

questions.question: What is the primary characteristic of an 'Outcome'?

options.text: It is a technical specification
It describes how well the product works
It is a measurable business or personal transformation (Time, Money, Risk)
It is the brand's mission statement

questions.explanation: Outcomes are about the result for the customer, typically measured in terms of time saved, money made/saved, or risk reduced. This is what prospects actually buy.

questions.question: What is the 'Dollar/Clock' test mentioned in the lesson?

options.text: A method for timing how long a sales call lasts
Checking if the customer can measure the result in Time or Money
A way to calculate the cost of development
Comparing your price to the competitor's clock speed

questions.explanation: If a benefit cannot be measured with a 'dollar sign' (money) or a 'clock icon' (time), you haven't yet reached the 'Outcome' level of messaging.

questions.question: Why is selling Outcomes better than selling Features for high-ticket products?

options.text: It makes the product seem more complex
It allows you to anchor the price to the value created, not the cost of software
It prevents competitors from stealing your technical secrets
Outcomes are easier for developers to build

questions.explanation: When you sell outcomes, you become a partner rather than a commodity. You can justify a higher price because that price is relative to the massive value or savings the customer achieves.

questions.question: In the 'Transformation Bridge' framework for creators, what should 80% of your messaging focus on?

options.text: The modules and templates in your course
Point A (Current Hell) and Point B (Desired Heaven)
Your personal credentials and experience
The price and payment options

questions.explanation: The Transformation Bridge teaches that you should spend 80% of your messaging on the pain (Point A) and the destination (Point B), and only 20% on the 'how' (your offer). Let them FEEL the transformation first.

questions.question: Pick one core feature of your product. Translate it through the three stages: Feature → Benefit → Outcome. Ensure the outcome passes the 'Dollar/Clock' test.
questions.promptForAI: Evaluate the student's ability to translate a feature into a measurable outcome. Check if the final outcome clearly mentions time saved, money gained/saved, or risk reduced. Provide feedback if they get stuck at the 'Benefit' level.
